title: Washington's Presidency and the Precedents He Set

steps.subgoalLabel: Understand why Washington's presidency mattered beyond politics
steps.instruction: George Washington was the first president (1789-1797). Why was everything he did especially important?
steps.work: As the first president, Washington had no examples to follow. Every decision he made — how to be addressed, who to appoint, when to step down — set a precedent (a model) for every future president.
steps.explanation: The Constitution described the president's powers but didn't cover many practical details. Washington's choices filled in those gaps and shaped the presidency for over 200 years.

steps.instruction: What specific precedents did Washington set?
steps.work: He created the Cabinet (group of advisors including Secretary of State and Treasury). He insisted on being called 'Mr. President' instead of a royal title. Most importantly, he voluntarily stepped down after two terms.
steps.explanation: Stepping down after two terms was Washington's most important precedent. In a world of kings who ruled for life, a leader voluntarily giving up power showed that the republic could transfer power peacefully.

steps.subgoalLabel: See the first political divisions emerge
steps.instruction: Washington's own Cabinet members disagreed sharply. What were they fighting about?
steps.work: Alexander Hamilton (Treasury) wanted a strong national bank, federal debt assumption, and a manufacturing economy. Thomas Jefferson (State) wanted limited federal power, state authority, and an agricultural economy. Their supporters became the first political parties: Federalists and Democratic-Republicans.
steps.explanation: The debate between Hamilton and Jefferson established a division that still echoes in American politics: how much power should the federal government have versus the states?

steps.subgoalLabel: Understand the peaceful transfer of power
steps.instruction: What happened when Washington left office and John Adams became president?
steps.work: In 1797, Washington peacefully handed power to John Adams after an election. In 1800, Adams lost to Jefferson. Despite bitter rivalry, Adams left office and Jefferson took over — the first transfer of power between opposing parties.
steps.explanation: The peaceful transfer of power between rivals in 1800 proved the American system worked. Leaders who lost elections actually stepped aside, which was rare in the world at that time.

title: How Hamilton's Financial Plan Built the Nation's Economy

steps.subgoalLabel: Understand the economic crisis facing the new nation
steps.instruction: After the Revolutionary War, the United States was deep in debt. What was the financial situation?
steps.work: The federal government owed about $54 million. Individual states owed another $25 million. There was no national currency, no national bank, and no reliable way to collect taxes. Foreign countries doubted America could pay its debts.
steps.explanation: A country that can't manage its debts loses credibility with other nations and can't borrow money when it needs to. The financial crisis threatened the survival of the new government.

steps.instruction: Treasury Secretary Alexander Hamilton proposed a bold plan. What were its three main parts?
steps.work: 1) The federal government would take over all state debts, paying them back in full to establish good credit. 2) Create a national bank to manage government money and issue stable currency. 3) Place tariffs (taxes on imported goods) to raise revenue and protect American manufacturing.
steps.explanation: Each part served a purpose: debt assumption tied states to the federal government, the bank created financial stability, and tariffs funded the government while encouraging American industry.

steps.instruction: Why did Jefferson and others oppose Hamilton's plan?
steps.work: Jefferson argued the Constitution didn't specifically authorize a national bank, so creating one was illegal. Southern states had already paid most of their war debts and didn't want to pay for Northern debts. Many feared Hamilton's plan would make the federal government too powerful.
steps.explanation: This debate raised a question that still matters: should the government only do what the Constitution explicitly allows (strict interpretation), or can it do things the Constitution doesn't forbid (loose interpretation)?

steps.subgoalLabel: See the result and its significance
steps.instruction: How was the disagreement resolved, and what happened?
steps.work: Hamilton, Jefferson, and Madison made a deal: Southern states would support the financial plan if the national capital was moved south to a new city on the Potomac River (Washington, D.C.). Hamilton's plan passed, and the economy stabilized.
steps.explanation: This deal showed how American politics worked from the very beginning — through negotiation and compromise. Hamilton's financial system gave the new nation economic credibility and a foundation for growth.
